description: Explains how marimo connects its notebook-aware AI assistant, MCP server and client support, external agent pairing, and shared tool registration model.
document:
marimo treats AI assistance as a notebook-aware editing feature rather than only a text completion layer. The important distinction is that marimo's assistant can work from notebook code and, when the user explicitly tags variables in a prompt, values from the running kernel such as dataframe or database schema information. This makes the assistant useful for data workflows where code generation depends on live notebook state rather than only static source text.

The editor exposes this assistance through several paths. A user can generate or refactor cells inside the notebook, use the chat panel, configure providers in settings, or create a whole notebook from the command line with `marimo new`. The chat panel has three modes that separate risk and capability. Manual mode uses only the conversation and user-provided context. Ask mode adds read-only marimo tools and tools from configured MCP client servers so the assistant can inspect notebooks. Agent mode includes the Ask mode tools and also enables editing tools that can add, remove, update, and run cells. This mode split is the main safety boundary in the editor AI experience.

MCP support connects that same notebook-aware model to other clients. The MCP guide says marimo can act as a server that exposes its AI tools to external MCP-compatible applications, and as a client that adds supported external MCP server tools to the marimo chat panel. Running the editor with MCP dependencies and the server flag mounts an endpoint for external clients; removing that flag still allows MCP client features. The docs call the feature experimental and warn that disabling token authentication is only appropriate for local development, because the server endpoint gives access to notebook data and tool behavior.

The implementation keeps backend chat tools and MCP tools unified. `ToolBase` gives each tool a typed argument model, typed output, notebook state context, and adapters for backend and MCP registration. Its call path coerces raw arguments into the tool's dataclass, preserves intentional structured tool errors, and wraps unexpected failures consistently. The backend adapter prepares a tool definition and validator for chat use, while the MCP adapter returns an annotated callable whose dataclass output is converted to plain data for MCP clients. The shared registry in `tools_registry.py` is therefore the handoff point: a tool added there is available both to marimo's chat panel and to the MCP server.

`ToolContext` is the bridge between AI tools and the running marimo application. It retrieves active sessions from the server state, resolves a notebook session by session ID, gathers cell notifications, and summarizes notebook or cell errors in notebook order. Tools rely on this context instead of reaching into application state independently, which keeps session lookup, missing-session errors, and error extraction consistent. The developer guide reinforces this pattern: define dataclass inputs and outputs, put shared notebook access in the context, use structured tool errors for expected failures, register the tool once, and add serialization tests plus user-facing documentation.

The MCP server registers the shared tools and a small set of prompts into a FastMCP app mounted under marimo's `/mcp` route. It uses the same application context as backend tools, requires edit scope through middleware, and can keep DNS rebinding protection enabled unless remote proxy use requires relaxing it. The prompts layer is separate from tools: prompt classes return MCP prompt messages such as active notebooks or summaries of current notebook errors. A separate code-mode MCP server supports pairing by exposing session listing and scratchpad execution, letting an external agent run temporary code against notebook variables without changing cells or the dependency graph.

For external agent collaboration, the generate-with-AI docs recommend the marimo pair skill for agent CLIs such as Claude Code, Codex, and OpenCode. That path is aimed at deeper notebook collaboration: reading variables, testing logic in a scratchpad, running cells, editing cells, and manipulating UI elements. The docs also encourage giving agents marimo-specific instructions and using file watching when an agent edits files on disk. Together, the editor AI, MCP integration, and pairing guidance form a layered design: built-in AI handles local generation and chat, shared tools provide notebook context, MCP opens those tools to compatible clients, and pairing skills teach external agents how to work productively with live marimo notebooks.
